summary: An indigenous North African ethnic group, known by the endonym Amazigh, whose homeland ("Tamazgha") stretches from Morocco to Egypt and south into the Sahara. Clues most often identify them through their Tamazight language and Tifinagh script, their major tribal confederations (Kabyle, Sanhaja, Zenata, Masmuda, Tuareg), and their long history of resisting and adapting to outside conquerors -- ancient Rome and Egypt, the Umayyad Arab conquest (resisted by the leaders Kusaila and the queen Kahina), and French colonialism. Berber dynasties, the Almoravids and Almohads, built empires stretching into Spain, and the nomadic Tuareg subgroup of the Sahara remains a distinct and frequently-clued people in their own right.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: An indigenous ethnic group of North Africa whose exonym "Berber" derives from the Latin/Greek "barbaros" and is now often considered pejorative; they call themselves Amazigh and their traditional homeland "Tamazgha," stretching from Morocco to Egypt and south across the Sahara. They speak the Tamazight language, historically written in the Tifinagh script (a "Neo-Tifinagh" alphabet was adopted by Morocco's king in 2003, and Tamazight became an official language of Morocco in 2011). The group is divided into numerous tribal subgroups and confederations -- including the Kabyle, Chaoui, Shilha, and Riffians, and historically the powerful Sanhaja, Zenata, Kutama, and Masmuda confederations -- and today is concentrated in the Rif and Atlas mountains, though they are also found in Libya's Fezzan region and considered the closest living relatives of the Canary Islands' Guanche people.

clues.clue: An indigenous ethnic group of North Africa; the exonym "Berber" derives from the Latin/Greek word "barbaros"
examples: what ethnicity is the main group indigenous to North Africa?
Name this broad ethnic group indigenous to North Africa. Their name is derived from the Latin word "barbaros."

clues.clue: Their endonym is "Amazigh"; "Berber" is an exonym often considered pejorative
examples: This group's endonym is Amazigh.
"Berber" is an exonym and often considered pejorative.

clues.clue: Speak the Tamazight language, historically written in the Tifinagh script; a "Neo-Tifinagh" alphabet was adopted by Morocco's king in 2003, and the language gained official/co-official status in Morocco in 2011
examples: In 2011, this people's language, which is written with the Tifinagh script, was elevated to official status by Mohammed VI of Morocco.
name these people who speak the Tamazight language, which is co-official in Morocco.

clues.clue: Live in a homeland they call "Tamazgha," stretching from Morocco to Egypt and south across the Sahara

clues.clue: The Imdlawn Tashlhiyt dialect of their language uniquely allows any consonant to serve as a syllable nucleus
examples: The Imdlawn Tashlhiyt dialect of their language allows any consonant to be a syllable nucleus.

clues.clue: Divided into numerous tribal subgroups and confederations -- including the Kabyle, Chaoui, and Shilha, and historically the Sanhaja, Zenata, Kutama, and Masmuda confederations -- concentrated today in the Rif and Atlas mountains
examples: This ethnicity is today mostly found in the Rif and Atlas mountain ranges.
Subgroups of this ethnicity include the Chaoui, Shilha, and the Kabyle.

clues.clue: Gave rise to the term "Barbary pirates"; the English pirate Jack Ward later introduced square-rigged ships to them
examples: name this North African ethnic group that inspired the name of Barbary pirates.
English pirate Jack Ward introduced square-rigged ships to this people.

clues.clue: Considered the closest living relatives of the Guanche, the indigenous people of the Canary Islands
examples: who are the closest living relatives of the Guanche.
Genetic and archaeological analyses show that the Guanche people were closely related to this ethnic group.

clues.clue: Long-time producers of Morocco's prized argan oil (harvested by women's cooperatives in the Sous region), forming a majority of Agadir's population; the Fezzan region of Libya, including the city of Zuwara and the Nufasa mountains, is also considered a historical homeland
examples: Morocco's argan oil has long been produced by members of this community, who make up a majority in Agadir.
Much of the GMMR's supply comes from the Fezzan region, thought to be the historical home of this ethnic group.

clues.clue: Were the primary inhabitants of Morocco's historically anarchic, ungoverned regions known as "bilad as-siba"
examples: This ethnicity was the primary inhabitant of the anarchic bilad as-siba.

works.name: Numidia and Roman North Africa
works.description: The Massylii subgroup was united by Masinissa into the Kingdom of Numidia, a Roman client kingdom that later included Volubilis, capital of a kingdom ruled by client-king Juba II, within a Roman province divided into regions including "Tingitana" and "Caesariensis." Masinissa captured his rival Syphax after aiding Rome at the Battle of the Great Plains, while decades later King Jugurtha was paraded by Gaius Marius in a triumph after the Jugurthine War; some later Numidians were heretical Christian martyrs who formed violent bands called the Circumcellions, and the author Apuleius presented an "Apology" at Sabratha against accusations of witchcraft. In the Vandal-Byzantine era, a kingdom of these people split into eight successor kingdoms, including Altava, after the defeat of its last ruler, Garmul, while an earlier king allied with Stotzas was opposed by the Byzantine governor John Troglita.

clues.clue: The Massylii subgroup was united by Masinissa into the Kingdom of Numidia
examples: The Tifinagh script is used by these people, whose Massylii subgroup were united by Massinissa into the Kingdom of Numidia.

clues.clue: Were the primary inhabitants of a Roman province divided into regions including "Tingitana" and "Caesariensis," and populated Volubilis, capital of a kingdom ruled by client-king Juba II
examples: These people were the primary inhabitants of a Roman province divided into regions partly named "Tingitana" and "Caesariensis." These people populated Volubilis, the capital of a Kingdom ruled by the client-king Juba II.

clues.clue: After aiding Rome at the Battle of the Great Plains, the Numidian king Masinissa captured his rival, Syphax
examples: After aiding the Romans at the Battle of the Great Plains, one king of this ethnicity captured Syphax.

clues.clue: Gaius Marius paraded King Jugurtha in a triumph after the namesake Jugurthine War
examples: Gaius Marius paraded a king of this ethnicity during a triumph after that king's namesake war.

clues.clue: Some were heretical Christian martyrs who formed violent bands called the Circumcellions
examples: Heretical Christian members of this ethnicity that prized martyrdom formed violent bands called Circumcellions.

clues.clue: The author Apuleius, one of their number, presented an "Apology" at Sabratha against accusations of using witchcraft to woo a wealthy woman
examples: An author of this ethnicity presented an Apology in Sabratha against accusations of using witchcraft to woo a wealthy woman.

clues.clue: A kingdom of these people split into eight successor kingdoms, including Altava, after the defeat of its last ruler, Garmul
examples: A kingdom of these people split into eight kingdoms, including Altava, after the defeat of its last ruler Garmul.

clues.clue: An earlier king, allied with Stotzas, was opposed by the Byzantine governor John Troglita
examples: A king of this ethnic group and his ally, Stotzas, were opposed by Governor John Troglita.

works.name: The Meshwesh and the Libyan Dynasties of Egypt
works.description: As the "Tjehnu," "Meshwesh," "Ma," or "Libu," ancient Libyan ancestors of the Berbers repeatedly fought Egypt's New Kingdom pharaohs; the Great Karnak Inscription records the defeat of the chieftain Meryey's alliance with the Lukka and the Sea Peoples, and the Merneptah Stele -- one of the first documents to mention "Israel" -- commemorates the same victory. They later ruled Egypt directly: the 22nd and 23rd Dynasties were ruled by people the Egyptians called the "Meshwesh." Some Berbers still date their calendar era to the accession of a king identified with the biblical Shishak -- the pharaoh Shoshenq I, founder of the 22nd Dynasty -- dividing the year into four "Gates of the Year," so that 2019 CE corresponded to year 2969 in this calendar.

clues.clue: As the "Tjehnu"/"Meshwesh"/"Ma"/"Libu," ancient Libyan ancestors fought Egypt's New Kingdom pharaohs; the Great Karnak Inscription and the Merneptah Stele (an early document mentioning "Israel") commemorate a victory over the chieftain Meryey
examples: Seti I fought a war against these people, whom he called the "Tjehnu," and the Great Karnak Inscription describes the defeat of their "Great Chief" Meryey's alliance with the Lukka and four Sea Peoples.

clues.clue: The 22nd and 23rd Dynasties of Egypt were ruled by people the Egyptians called the "Meshwesh"
examples: The 22nd and 23rd dynasties of Egypt were ruled by people whom the Egyptians called Meshwesh, likely of this ethnic group from Libya.

clues.clue: Some date their calendar era from the accession of a king identified with the biblical Shishak -- Shoshenq I -- dividing the year into four "Gates of the Year" (2019 CE = year 2969)
examples: Some members of this ethnicity date their calendar era from the accession of Shoshenq I, so that 2019 is year 2969.
These people's calendar starts from the rule of a king identified with the biblical Shishak and is divided by four "Gates of the Year."

works.name: The Garamantes
works.description: A desert civilization of the Fezzan region of Libya, famed for building an extensive network of underground aqueducts; their distinctive pottery, marked by roulette decoration, provides the northernmost evidence of that technique in Africa. They supported the Berber leader Tacfarinas's revolt against Rome, and were attacked in 19 BCE by Lucius Cornelius Balbus during the first Roman trans-Saharan expedition; the Limes Tripolitanus was built partly to defend against them, and their capital, Garama, was captured in 202 CE by Emperor Septimius Severus.

clues.clue: Famed for building an extensive network of aqueducts in the Fezzan region of Libya
examples: A group of these people known as the "Garamantes" were famed for building aqueducts.
These people made aqueducts in the Fezzan region of Libya and supported Tacfarinas's revolt against the Romans.

clues.clue: Their distinctive roulette-decorated pottery provides the northernmost evidence of that technique in Africa
examples: Later pottery named for these people provides the northernmost evidence of roulette decoration in Africa.

clues.clue: Attacked in 19 BCE by Lucius Cornelius Balbus during the first Roman trans-Saharan expedition; the Limes Tripolitanus was built partly to defend against them
examples: Lucius Cornelius Balbus led the first trans-Saharan expedition, during which he attacked these people in the Fezzan in 19 BCE. The Limes Tripolitanus defended against these people.

clues.clue: Their capital, Garama, was captured in 202 CE by Emperor Septimius Severus, who then expanded the Limes Tripolitanus
examples: After capturing the Garamantian capital of Garama in 202 CE, this emperor expanded the Limes Tripolitanus.

works.name: Kusaila and Kahina: Resistance to the Umayyad Conquest
works.description: The Christian Berber king Kusaila killed the Umayyad general Uqba ibn Nafi at the Battle of Vescera in 682, but was killed five years later near Mems resisting an invasion led by Zuhayr ibn Qays. He was succeeded as leader of Berber resistance by the queen Kahina (also called Dihya), a member of the Zenata confederation whose faith is disputed -- Ibn Khaldun recorded a tradition that she was Jewish, though other accounts suggest Christian or pagan roots, including a story that she carried a large wooden idol on a camel, suggesting a conversion from Judaism to Christianity. Ruling from the Aures region and, according to legend, able to see into the future, Kahina won the decisive Battle of Meskiana (698) against the Umayyad general Hassan ibn al-Nu'man, practiced scorched-earth tactics by destroying her own people's cities, and fortified the Roman amphitheater at El Djem, Tunisia as a stronghold -- but was ultimately defeated by the Umayyad general Musa bin Nusayr, ending organized Berber resistance to the conquest.

clues.clue: The Christian Berber king Kusaila killed the Umayyad general Uqba ibn Nafi at the Battle of Vescera (682), but was killed five years later near Mems resisting an invasion led by Zuhayr ibn Qays
examples: Gabriel Camps created an encyclopedia related to these people, who were led by a warrior who killed Uqba ibn Nafi at the Battle of Vescera.
A Christian leader of these people named Kusaila was killed near Mems while resisting an invasion led by Zuhayr ibn Qays.

clues.clue: Kahina succeeded Kusaila as leader of Berber resistance and won the decisive Battle of Meskiana (698) against the Umayyad general Hassan ibn al-Nu'man
examples: Kusaila was succeeded as a leader of these people by a possibly Jewish queen who won the Battle of Meskiana, Kahina.

This queen from the Zenata succeeded Kusaila as leader of the Berber resistance. Although her victory at the 698 Battle of Meskiana was absolute, whether she was pagan, Christian, or Jewish remains uncertain to this day.

clues.clue: A member of the Zenata confederation of disputed faith -- Ibn Khaldun recorded a tradition that she was Jewish, though other accounts suggest Christian or pagan roots, including a story that she carried a large wooden idol on a camel, suggesting conversion from Judaism to Christianity
examples: Ibn Khaldun recorded the Jewish faith of a queen of this ethnicity, Kahina, who belonged to the Zenata confederation.
According to Abu Bakr al-Maliki, this woman carried around a large wooden idol on a camel, suggesting she had converted from Judaism to Christianity.

clues.clue: Ruled from the Aures region and, per legend, could see into the future; supposedly lived to be 127 years old
examples: Name this queen of the Aures who supposedly could see into the future and used the El Djem amphitheater as a fortress during a revolt against the Umayyads.
A queen of this people named Dihya supposedly lived to be 127 years old and led the failed resistance against Musa bin Nusayr.

clues.clue: Practiced scorched-earth tactics by destroying her own people's cities to deny resources to the invaders, and fortified the Roman amphitheater at El Djem, Tunisia as a stronghold
examples: According to Islamic historians, a leader of these people destroyed their cities in an attempt to resist the Muslim conquest.
Dihya's forces fortified one of these structures at modern-day El Djem, Tunisia.

clues.clue: Was ultimately defeated by the Umayyad general Musa bin Nusayr, ending organized Berber resistance to the conquest
examples: A queen of this people named Dihya supposedly lived to be 127 years old and led the failed resistance against Musa bin Nusayr.

works.name: Tariq ibn Ziyad and the Conquest of Spain
works.description: The Berber general Tariq ibn Ziyad led the Umayyad conquest of al-Andalus in 711, giving his name to the Strait of Gibraltar ("Jabal Tariq," Tariq's Mountain). He defeated the Visigothic King Roderic at the decisive Battle of Guadalete, a victory that led to the capture of the Visigothic capital, Toledo -- later displaced by Seville and then Cordoba as the center of al-Andalus.

clues.clue: Led the 711 Umayyad conquest of al-Andalus, giving his name to the Strait of Gibraltar
examples: The Umayyad conquest of al-Andalus was led by one of these people, Tariq ibn Ziyad, who also names the Strait of Gibraltar.
A member of this ethnic group led the Umayyad conquest of al-Andalus and gave his name to the Strait of Gibraltar.

clues.clue: Defeated the Visigothic King Roderic at the decisive Battle of Guadalete
examples: The Kahina was one of these people, another of whom led an army that defeated the forces of the Visigothic King Roderic at the Battle of Guadalete and lent his name to Gibraltar.

clues.clue: Victory at Guadalete led to the capture of the Visigothic capital, Toledo
examples: Victory at the Battle of Guadalete resulted in the capture of this city by Tariq ibn Ziyad.

works.name: The Great Berber Revolt (739-743)
works.description: Originally led by Maysara, this revolt failed to capture the city of Kairouan; it was inspired partly by preachers of the Kharijite sect, and its fighters -- who made up the majority of the Umayyad army in Iberia -- once tied bags of water to the tails of mares to provoke a stampede that neutralized an enemy cavalry force sent by Caliph Hisham. The revolt precipitated the Umayyad withdrawal from northern Spain, allowing the Christian Kingdom of Asturias to survive.

clues.clue: Originally led by Maysara; failed to capture the city of Kairouan
examples: This ethnic group failed to capture Kairouan in a 740 revolt originally led by Maysara.

clues.clue: Fighters tied bags of water to the tails of mares to provoke a stampede that neutralized an enemy cavalry force sent by Caliph Hisham
examples: To defeat an expedition ordered by caliph Hisham, people of this ethnic group tied bags of water to the tails of mares, provoking a stampede that allowed them to neutralize cavalry.

clues.clue: Inspired partly by preachers of the Kharijite sect
examples: Later, the Umayyads faced the Great Berber Revolt in the 740s, inspired by preachers from this sect.

clues.clue: Fighters made up the majority of the Umayyad army in Iberia; the revolt precipitated the Umayyad withdrawal from northern Spain, allowing the Kingdom of Asturias to survive
examples: A 739-43 revolt of these nomadic North African people, who had made up the majority of the Umayyad army, precipitated the withdrawal of Umayyad forces from northern Spain, allowing the survival of the Kingdom of Asturias.

works.name: The Almoravid and Almohad Empires
works.description: Religious leaders within the Sanhaja confederation, the Berbers' largest tribal confederation, encouraged the formation of the Almoravid Empire, whose name derives from desert frontier fortresses called "ribat"; the dynasty was nicknamed "the muffled ones" for the veils its members wore over their mouths. The Almoravids controlled the oasis city of Sijilmasa, the northern terminus of the medieval trans-Saharan trade route linking them to the salt mines of Taghaza, and may have sacked Kumbi Saleh, capital of the Ghana Empire; an Almoravid emir won the Battle of Sagrajas (1086) after being invited by the taifa princes of al-Andalus to defend against King Alfonso VI of Castile, leading to the taifas' incorporation into the dynasty. Ibn Tumart later founded the Almohad movement by uniting the Masmuda tribe (distinct from the Zanata and Sanhaja), overthrowing the ruling Almoravids; the resulting Almohad Caliphate lost to Alfonso VIII of Castile at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) and was ultimately destroyed when its last leader, Idris II, was assassinated and the Marinids seized its capital, Marrakech. Berbers of these dynasties also carried Islam south to Senegal.

clues.clue: Religious leaders within the Sanhaja confederation, the Berbers' largest, encouraged the formation of the Almoravid Empire, named for desert frontier fortresses called "ribat"
examples: Religious leaders within the Sanhaja, this ethnicity's largest tribal confederation, encouraged the formation of the Almoravid Empire.
Another empire ruled by this people, which was named for "ribat" frontier fortresses, supposedly sacked Kumbi Saleh.

clues.clue: The Almoravid dynasty was nicknamed "the muffled ones" for the veils its members wore over their mouths
examples: A dynasty founded by members of this ethnicity was nicknamed "the muffled ones" for the veils they wore over their mouths.

clues.clue: Controlled the oasis city of Sijilmasa, northern terminus of the trans-Saharan trade route to the salt mines of Taghaza, and may have sacked Kumbi Saleh, capital of the Ghana Empire
examples: The trade routes whose northern terminus was in the city of Sijilmasa linked this people to the salt mines of Taghaza.
These people controlled Sijilmasa, the northern terminus of medieval trans-Saharan trade.

clues.clue: An Almoravid emir won the Battle of Sagrajas (1086) after being invited by the taifa princes to defend against King Alfonso VI of Castile, leading to the taifas' incorporation into the dynasty
examples: An emir of this ethnicity won the Battle of Sagrajas after being invited by the taifa princes to defend against Alfonso VI, leading to those taifa being incorporated into that emir's Almoravid dynasty.

clues.clue: Ibn Tumart founded the Almohad movement by uniting the Masmuda tribe (distinct from the Zanata and Sanhaja), overthrowing the ruling Almoravids; the Almohad Caliphate later lost to Alfonso VIII at Las Navas de Tolosa and was destroyed when its last leader, Idris II, was assassinated and the Marinids seized Marrakech
examples: Ibn Tumart started a religious movement among the Masmuda subgroup of these people who overthrew the ruling Almoravids.
name this North African ethnic group that composed the Almohad Dynasty.

clues.clue: Carried Islam south to Senegal, having also established the Almoravid and Almohad kingdoms in Morocco
examples: Islam was brought to Senegal by these indigenous peoples of North Africa, who established the Almoravid and Almohad kingdoms in Morocco.

works.name: The Tuareg
works.description: A nomadic, Tamasheq-speaking subgroup of the Berbers found across the Sahara, traditionally called the "Blue People" for their indigo-dyed "tagelmust" veils and known for matrilineal, matrilocal customs; they are organized into confederations called "kels" (named, for instance, for the Hoggar Mountains) led by chiefs called "amenokal," with subunits known as "drum-groups." Their ancestress, the 4th-century CE queen Tin Hinan, has a tomb at Abalessa that was controversially excavated in 1925 by the amateur archaeologist and tomb raider Count Byron de Prorok; the missionary Charles de Foucauld later lived among them, and an Ottoman-backed rebellion during World War I was led by Kaocen. In 2012, the secular MNLA and the Islamist Ansar Dine (founded by Iyad ag Ghaly) fought to make the northern Malian region of Azawad -- including the cities of Gao and Timbuktu -- an independent Tuareg state, prompting France's Operation Serval and Operation Barkhane; the largest Tuareg population lives in Mali. Other rebellions include a 2007 Nigerien uprising protesting uranium mining at Arlit and a 2015 insurgency (marked by a Radisson Blu hotel attack) born from conflict with the Toubou that produced the band Tinariwen, as well as a 2023 dispute with Russia's Wagner Group. The Tuareg also opposed Muammar Gaddafi during the Libyan Civil War, and director Abderrahmane Sissako's film Timbuktu fictionalizes the execution of a Tuareg cattle herder.

clues.clue: A nomadic, Tamasheq-speaking Saharan people, traditionally called the "Blue People" for indigo-dyed "tagelmust" veils and known for matrilineal, matrilocal customs; organized into confederations called "kels" (e.g. named for the Hoggar Mountains) led by chiefs called "amenokal," with subunits called "drum-groups"
examples: name this nomadic Berber group of the Sahara Desert.
One people of this ethnicity have been named the 'Blue People' for their indigo-dyed veils and are exceptionally matrilineal and matrilocal.

clues.clue: Their ancestress, the 4th-century CE queen Tin Hinan, has a tomb at Abalessa controversially excavated in 1925 by the amateur archaeologist and tomb raider Count Byron de Prorok
examples: In 1925, a monumental tomb constructed by these people for Queen Tin Hinan was controversially excavated by amateur archaeologist and tomb raider Count Byron de Prorok.
The tomb of these peoples' 4th-century CE queen Tin Hanan was excavated in Abalessa.

clues.clue: The Catholic missionary Charles de Foucauld (recently canonized a saint) lived among them; during World War I an Ottoman-backed rebellion was led by Kaocen, considered (like the later Mano Dayak) a martyr
examples: The recently-canonized saint Charles de Foucauld lived among these people and noted how their amenokal led tribes divided into "drum-groups". During World War I, an Ottoman-backed rebellion of these people was led by Kaocen.

clues.clue: In 2012, the secular MNLA and Islamist Ansar Dine (founded by Iyad ag Ghaly) fought to make the northern Malian region of Azawad -- including Gao and Timbuktu -- an independent Tuareg state; France's Operation Serval and Operation Barkhane sought to end the insurgency, and the largest Tuareg population lives in Mali
examples: The MNLA and Ansar Dine fought for control in a 2012 rebellion named for these people that began after a coup targeting Amadou Toure.
The breakaway state of Azawad was formed by these people, whose men wear indigo-colored tagelmust veils.

clues.clue: Other rebellions include a 2007 Nigerien uprising protesting uranium mining at Arlit and a 2015 insurgency (marked by a Radisson Blu hotel attack) born from conflict with the Toubou that produced the band Tinariwen; in 2023 Tuareg rebels blamed a Wagner Group attack that killed two men
examples: A 2007 rebellion of these people protested the impacts of mining in Arlit and a lack of action on demands made by Mano Dayak a decade earlier.
In August of 2023, Tuareg rebels blamed this private military group for an attack that killed two men.

clues.clue: Director Abderrahmane Sissako's film Timbuktu fictionalizes the execution of a Tuareg cattle herder in its title city
examples: Abderrahmane Sissako's most famous film fictionalizes the execution of a cattle herder of this ethnicity in the title city.

clues.clue: Opposed Muammar Gaddafi's government during the Libyan Civil War
examples: Their Tuareg sub-group opposed Gaddafi during the Libyan Civil War.

works.name: Colonial Era and Modern Nationalism
works.description: French colonial ideology promoted the "Kabyle myth" (per Orientalist Amedee Caix de Saint-Aymour), which portrayed the Kabyle subgroup as more "hardworking, enterprising, practical," and less religious -- and thus easier to assimilate -- than Arabs; historian Natalya Vince argues the myth made French authorities skeptical that Berber women like Zohra Drif could join Algeria's War of Independence of their own will. Berber soldiers recruited into the French army after the conquest of Algeria, known for their baggy red trousers, were called "zouaves." In the 1920s, Abd el Krim led the Rif War (Second Moroccan War) against Spain -- whose Disaster of Annual saw General Manuel Fernandez Silvestre defeated in 1921 -- and was later invited back from exile by King Mohammed V, Morocco's first post-independence leader. A namesake "Berber Spring" of protests broke out in 1980 in Algeria's Kabylie region.

clues.clue: French colonial ideology promoted the "Kabyle myth," portraying the Kabyle subgroup as more assimilable than Arabs; historian Natalya Vince argues it made French authorities skeptical that Berber women like Zohra Drif could join Algeria's War of Independence of their own will
examples: Name these people whose subgroups include the Kabyle, who were subject to an imperial myth portraying them as ideal colonial subjects.

Natalya Vince argues that the Kabyle myth influenced French authorities to find it incredible that Berber women like Zohra Drif can join this North African country's independence war of their own will.

clues.clue: Berber soldiers recruited into the French army after the conquest of Algeria, known for baggy red trousers, were called "zouaves"
examples: After the French invasion of Algeria, many Berber soldiers were recruited into the French army. Those soldiers, known for their baggy red trousers, were known by this term.

clues.clue: Abd el Krim, a leader of this ethnic group (divided here into the Rif and Tuareg groups), led the Rif War (Second Moroccan War) against Spain -- whose Disaster of Annual saw General Manuel Fernandez Silvestre defeated in 1921 -- and was later invited back from exile by King Mohammed V, Morocco's first post-independence leader
examples: Abd el Krim was a member of this North African ethnic group, further divided into the Rif and Tuareg groups.

clues.clue: A namesake "Berber Spring" of protests broke out in 1980 in Algeria's Kabylie region
examples: This ethnicity had a namesake 'Spring' in 1980 in the Kabylie region of Algeria.

works.name: Scholarship and Cultural Memory
works.description: Pierre Bourdieu's ethnographic study of the Kabyle subgroup analyzed the "homologous oppositions" (such as man/woman) structuring their homes in a work titled The Kabyle House, or the World Reversed, while anthropologist Ernest Gellner, in Saints of the Atlas, discussed the authority of their hereditary "marabout" saints to argue for segmentary lineage theory. Clifford Geertz's Islam Observed compares the Berber saint Al-Yusi to the Javanese saint Kalijaga to analyze "the antipodes of the Muslim world." Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah -- whose full title also names the Arabs -- was the first to distinguish the Berber tribes and recounts the story of the queen Kahina, and historian Gabriel Camps created an encyclopedia devoted to the Berbers. According to the 17th-century historian Ahmed Mohammed al-Maqqari, a Berber polymath once attempted to fly by attaching feathers to himself, his only design flaw being a lack of a tail to smooth the landing.

clues.clue: Pierre Bourdieu's ethnographic study of the Kabyle subgroup analyzed the "homologous oppositions" (e.g. man/woman) structuring their homes, in a work titled The Kabyle House, or the World Reversed
examples: These people's homes are organized in "homologous oppositions" like "man to woman" according to a structuralist account titled "[this people's] House, or the World Reversed."
Pierre Bourdieu worked with the Kabyle subgroup of these people.

clues.clue: To argue for segmentary lineage theory, anthropologist Ernest Gellner (in Saints of the Atlas) discussed the authority of their hereditary "marabout" saints
examples: To argue for segmentary lineage theory, Ernest Gellner discussed the authority of this people's hereditary marabout saints.

clues.clue: Clifford Geertz's Islam Observed compares the Berber saint Al-Yusi to the Javanese saint Kalijaga to analyze "the antipodes of the Muslim world"
examples: The Javanese saint Kalijaga is compared to a saint of this ethnicity named Al-Yusi to analyze "the antipodes of the Muslim world" in Clifford Geertz's Islam Observed.

clues.clue: Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah -- whose full title also names the Arabs -- was the first to distinguish the Berber tribes and recounts the story of the queen Kahina
examples: Arabs and this ethnic group are mentioned in the full title of Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah.
ibn Khaldun wrote about Kahina, a religiously Jewish queen of these still-existent North African peoples.

clues.clue: Historian Gabriel Camps created an encyclopedia devoted to the Berbers
examples: Gabriel Camps created an encyclopedia related to these people.

clues.clue: According to the 17th-century historian Ahmed Mohammed al-Maqqari, a Berber polymath once attempted to fly by attaching feathers to himself, his only design flaw being a lack of a tail to smooth the landing
examples: 17th century historian Ahmed Mohammed al-Maqqari claimed that a polymath of this people was able to fly by attaching feathers to himself and that his only design flaw was not adding a tail to smooth the landing.

comprehensive_summary: The Berbers are an indigenous ethnic group of North Africa who call themselves Amazigh, live in a traditional homeland they call Tamazgha stretching from Morocco to Egypt and south across the Sahara, and speak the Tamazight language, historically written in the Tifinagh script (Tamazight became officially recognized in Morocco in 2011). They are divided into numerous tribal subgroups -- Kabyle, Chaoui, Shilha, Riffians, and historically the Sanhaja, Zenata, Kutama, and Masmuda confederations -- and are considered the closest living relatives of the Canary Islands' Guanche people. Their ancient history includes the Kingdom of Numidia, united by Masinissa (who captured his rival Syphax at the Battle of the Great Plains) and later ruled by Jugurtha (paraded by Gaius Marius after the Jugurthine War); some Numidians became heretical Christian Circumcellion martyrs, and the author Apuleius defended himself against witchcraft accusations at Sabratha. As the "Meshwesh" or "Tjehnu," ancient Libyan ancestors fought Egypt's New Kingdom pharaohs (commemorated on the Merneptah Stele) before ruling Egypt directly as its 22nd and 23rd Dynasties under Shoshenq I, whose accession still marks some Berbers' calendar era. In the Sahara, the Garamantes built extensive aqueducts in Libya's Fezzan region and resisted Roman expeditions under Balbus and Septimius Severus. Following the Arab conquest, Berber resistance was led first by the Christian king Kusaila (who killed Uqba ibn Nafi before his own death) and then by the queen Kahina, whose disputed Jewish, Christian, or pagan faith and scorched-earth tactics culminated in her defeat by Musa bin Nusayr; meanwhile Tariq ibn Ziyad led the 711 conquest of Spain, defeating King Roderic at Guadalete and naming the Strait of Gibraltar after himself, and a Great Berber Revolt (739-743), led by Maysara and partly inspired by Kharijite preachers, forced an Umayyad withdrawal from northern Spain. Berber dynasties later built major empires: the Almoravids, based on the Sanhaja confederation and nicknamed "the muffled ones," controlled the trans-Saharan trade hub of Sijilmasa and won the Battle of Sagrajas in Spain, before Ibn Tumart's Masmuda-based Almohad movement overthrew them and built a caliphate that fell to Alfonso VIII at Las Navas de Tolosa. The nomadic, Tamasheq-speaking Tuareg subgroup of the Sahara -- the "Blue People" of indigo tagelmust veils, organized into "kel" confederations under "amenokal" chiefs -- trace their ancestry to the 4th-century queen Tin Hinan and remain a distinct people, from Charles de Foucauld's era through the 2012 Azawad independence bid (fought by the MNLA and Ansar Dine) and subsequent conflicts with the Toubou, Gaddafi's government, and Russia's Wagner Group. In the colonial and modern era, France's "Kabyle myth" cast some Berbers as more assimilable than Arabs, Berber "zouave" soldiers served in the French army, Abd el Krim led the 1920s Rif War against Spain, and a 1980 "Berber Spring" broke out in Algeria's Kabylie region. Scholars including Pierre Bourdieu, Ernest Gellner, Clifford Geertz, Ibn Khaldun, and Gabriel Camps have all studied Berber society and history.
recursive_suggestions: Kahina (Dihya) -- dense clue volume plus two independent bonus-part answerlines (al-Kahina, Dihya); could eventually support a standalone figure page distinct from the Berbers as a whole

Tuareg -- appeared as its own tossup answerline twice (Tuareg, Tuaregs) with dense, largely-modern clue content (Azawad rebellions, Tin Hinan, Charles de Foucauld); strong candidate for a standalone topic
Numidians -- own tossup answerline with dense ancient content (Masinissa, Jugurtha, Circumcellions, Apuleius); consider a standalone topic
Garamantes -- own bonus-part answerline in two separate bonuses (aqueducts, Balbus's expedition, Septimius Severus); could support a standalone page with more data
Almohad Caliphate / Masmuda Berbers -- own bonus-part answerlines distinct from "Berbers"; the dynasty's founding, fall to Alfonso VIII, and destruction by the Marinids could support its own page
Ancient Libyans / Meshwesh -- own bonus-part answerline (Seti I, Great Karnak Inscription, Merneptah Stele); the Egyptian 22nd/23rd Dynasty connection could be developed as its own topic
Rif War -- own bonus-part answerline (Abd el Krim, Disaster of Annual, Manuel Fernandez Silvestre); could be developed into its own event page

Ibn Khaldun -- historian who wrote extensively about the Berbers and appeared as his own answerline across multiple bonuses (Muqaddimah, asabiyyah, cited by Peter Turchin); a Philosophy/History crossover candidate

Ambiguity flag: bonus parts about the Senegal River, the Qadiriyya Sufi order, al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah, Shi'a Islam, Toledo, King Roderick, Nero, the New Kingdom, the Great Man-Made River, modern Libya, Algeria, the FLN, argan oil, the Rif Mountains, Peter Turchin, Ibn Battuta, the Wagner Group, Mali, Zinder, the Senussi order, Mohammed V, and the Sahara Desert/Cave of Swimmers all surfaced as distinct answerlines within multi-part Berbers-adjacent bonuses and were excluded from card content -- filter to History + Berbers/Amazigh specifically when re-querying
